Patch Set 2:

> Unrelated, why does the automation re-run if nothing changes?
 > Patchset 1 and 2 are identical. That seems odd to me.

TBH, I don't know why Gerrit even allowed you to push a new version of the patch, since the commit message and code is the same, but it was just a new commit. 

In any case, the reason that testing is re-run is that there may be new Test-Parameters in the commit message so it creates a new build and test sessions on every commit.
Patch Set 2: Code-Review+1

Patch Set 2:

I think if I checkout the branch rather than cherry-picking the change, I can avoid creating a new patch set. Cherry-picking creates a new SHA for the commit, which Gerrit seems to recognize. At least that's what Stack Overflow says.

Patch Set 2:

Yes, definitely if you are pushing a new patch on top of an existing patch then you need to use checkout instead of cherry-pick. Normally the old patch is changed because of a different parent version, and Gerrit will detect if the patch is otherwise identical, but it didn't in this case.
Patch Set 2: Code-Review+1
Patch Set 2: Code-Review+2
Change has been successfully cherry-picked as edb968d04f3a3c8054c12daee1ba557f855055ce by Oleg Drokin

parents.subject: LU-16868 tests: skip conf-sanity/32 in interop

commit.subject: LU-17242 debug: remove CFS_CHECK_STACK
commit.message:
LU-17242 debug: remove CFS_CHECK_STACK

CFS_CHECK_STACK is primitive, doesn't work on
x86_64, and only dumps a stack in kernel log
when we are fairly close to passing the stack
limit anyway.

Admins and developers can grab the same info from
debug/tracing/stack_trace and debug/tracing/stack_max_size
on a live system. And the kernel will dump a stack
if it 'Oops' from going over the stack limit.

We don't need an additional Lustre specific stack
checking mechanism.

Test-Parameters: trivial
Signed-off-by: Timothy Day <timday@amazon.com>
Change-Id: Icc7c82f6a0dcd727de6ce2c2d40ba071ee349c0c
